feedback.text: It would be nice if the Digital Fairness Act included some legislation that forced producers to have a end of life plan for digital products. For an example: I currently own a game "Microsoft Flight Simulator 2020" which requires a internet connection to servers to fully load all the scenery. Currently I have no idea until when I can expect the game servers to be supported and so for the game to remain fully functional. On a completely separate note: another game called "Minecraft: Java Edition" is rated PEGI 7 and has access to multiplayer games on severs. Some of these servers ex.: play.purpleprison.net predominantly feature gambling although the game is rated for children and is against Mojang's, the game publishers, policy, which they completely fail to enforce. I believe that a game rated for children shouldn't under any circumstances feature gambling or any kind of access to it. Finally I also wanted to mention that the game "Minecraft for Windows" (not to be confused with "Minecraft: Java Edition") features a built-in marketplace on which players can buy for real money in the form of "Minecoins" different items. However there is no purchase confirmation or refunds, which means that people can accidentally spend a sizeable amount of money on stuff that they didn't want because of a misclick and with no option to get their money back. To me this seams pretty unfair. Also the fact that most of the content on this marketplace is stolen anyways is a different rabbit hole.
